coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infection in chronic wounds causes severe morbidity and mortality by prolonging the inflammation and proliferation phase during the healing process. Reported carbon nanomaterials can exert anti-microbial action; however, uncontrolled therapeutic response, cytotoxicity, and high dosage requirement have limited their potential. Toward the end, we have developed programmable carbon dot liposomes (CDsomes) from triolein through simple pyrolysis. CDsomes can achieve “on” and “off” states upon ultraviolet (UV; 385 nm) and green (532 nm) light irradiation, respectively. The “on” state CDsomes participate in a thermodynamically favorable photocatalytic reaction to produce hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which acts as a substrate for the “off” state CDsomes (⋅CDsomes – or ⋅CDsomes +; metastable radical state) to generate hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) via peroxidase-mimicking activity. The cascade reaction was utilized against bacteria, including multidrug-resistant bacteria. The practicality of CDsomes was demonstrated by treating the methicillin-resistant S. aureus infected wounds, CDsomes were pre-activated with UV light irradiation to avoid direct harmful exposure to tissues followed by in situ green light irradiation. This encouraged rapid healing by boosting the inflammatory immune responses owing to the intrinsic properties of self-preserved oleic acid. Light-triggered CDsomes with substantial biocompatibility holds great potential as an intelligent anti-microbial modality, particularly for chronic wound healing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation and optical properties of carbon dot liposomes (CDsomes). (A) Schematic representation of the pyrolysis of triolein followed by hydrolysis and self-assembly to form CDsomes. (B) TEM image of CDsomes. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDsomes. (D) Bright-field and fluorescence images of CDsomes recorded with various excitation wavelength filters using an inverted fluorescence microscope. (E) Fluorescence images of CDsomes excited sequentially at 360–390 nm and 525−545 nm to demonstrate photoswitching properties using an inverted fluorescence microscope. Scale bar in (D) is 10 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Dual light triggered ROS generation through programmable CDsomes and their possible mechanisms. (A) Fluorescence of AR (20.0 μM) after incubation with HRP (0.1 U mL−1) and/or CDsomes in the absence and presence of H2O2 (10 μM), 385- or 532-nm irradiation. The fluorescence intensities of AR-oxidized product (i.e., resorufin) were measured at an excitation/emission wavelength of 530/585 nm, respectively. (B) Fluorescence of NaTA (5.0 μM) recorded after incubation with CDsomes before and after irradiation with 385-, 532-, or sequential (385- and 532- nm) irradiation. The fluorescence intensities of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA; NaTA oxidized product) were recorded at excitation/emission maxima of 312/426 nm, respectively. (C) The ESR spectra of DMPO and CDsomes under different light irradiation in the absence and presence of catalase (12000 U mL−1). Black dots (●) in the EPR spectra correspond to DMPO–·OH (aN = aH = 14.9 G) adducts. The concentration of CDsomes was fixed at 100 μg mL−1. Parameters of light source used in (A)−(C) are as follows: UV light (385 nm, 2 mW cm−2, 10 min) and green light (532 nm, 150 mW cm−2, 1 min). (D) (a) CV curve for CDsomes was recorded in a wide potential window (Scan rate: 10 mV s−1) in the presence of an ionic liquid (tetrabutylammonium hexaﬂuorophosphate), an inert electrolyte solution. (b) Proposed electron transfer mechanism to achieve “on” and “off” states of CDsomes and their further role as a photocatalyst and nanozyme for the production of ROS upon photoirradiation. (E) Scheme of “on/off” state-dependent catalytic photoactivity of CDsomes. Asterisks in (A) and (B) indicate statistically significant differences of each group compared to those of AR + HRP and TA + CDsomes, respectively (*p < 0.05, ***p < 0.001). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Cascade reaction induced anti-microbial response of CDsomes. (A) Schematic representation of sequential photoirradiation assisted anti-microbial treatment. (B) Colony formation assays of E. coli on LB agar plates untreated and treated with CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) under 385-, 532-, or sequential (385 and 532-nm) light irradiation. (C) Comparative MIC90 values of CDsomes against two Gram-negative (left arrow) and two Gram-positive (right arrow) bacterial strains in the dark and under photoirradiation. Parameters of light source used in (B) and (C) are as follows: UV light (385 nm, 2 mW cm−2, 10 min) and green light (532 nm, 150 mW cm−2, 1 min). Asterisks in (C) indicate statistically significant differences of groups treated with CDsomes under photoirradiation compared to those of treated with CDsomes without light, respectively (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Light-driven CDsomes-based therapy for wound healing. (A) (a) Digital photographs and (b) relative wound area, (B) (a) representative colony formation and (b) relative bacterial viabilities of MRSA collected from the wound site on day 9, untreated and treated with a commercial 3 M bandage, Ag NPs ([Ag] = 100 μg mL−1], and 100 μg mL−1 of CDsomes without and with 385-, 532-, or sequential (385 and 532-nm) light irradiation. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three repeated measurements. (C) H&E and Mason trichrome staining of tissue specimens isolated on postoperative day 9 from the untreated and treated wounds. The parameters of the light source are as follows: UV light (385 nm, 2 mW cm−2, 10 min) and green light (532 nm, 150 mW cm−2, 1 min). Red and yellow lines represent the thickness of the epidermis and dermis, respectively. Scale bar in (A) and (C) are 50 mm and 100 μm, respectively. Asterisks in (A-b) and (B-b) indicate statistically significant differences of each group compared to those of untreated ones (**p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Light triggered programmable states of carbon dot liposomes accelerate chronic wound healing via photocatalytic cascade reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infection in chronic wounds causes severe morbidity and mortality by prolonging the inflammation and proliferation phase during the healing process. Reported carbon nanomaterials can exert anti-microbial action; however, uncontrolled therapeutic response, cytotoxicity, and high dosage requirement have limited their potential. Toward the end, we have developed programmable carbon dot liposomes (CDsomes) from triolein through simple pyrolysis. CDsomes can achieve “on” and “off” states upon ultraviolet (UV; 385 nm) and green (532 nm) light irradiation, respectively. The “on” state CDsomes participate in a thermodynamically favorable photocatalytic reaction to produce hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which acts as a substrate for the “off” state CDsomes (⋅CDsomes – or ⋅CDsomes +; metastable radical state) to generate hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) via peroxidase-mimicking activity. The cascade reaction was utilized against bacteria, including multidrug-resistant bacteria. The practicality of CDsomes was demonstrated by treating the methicillin-resistant S. aureus infected wounds, CDsomes were pre-activated with UV light irradiation to avoid direct harmful exposure to tissues followed by in situ green light irradiation. This encouraged rapid healing by boosting the inflammatory immune responses owing to the intrinsic properties of self-preserved oleic acid. Light-triggered CDsomes with substantial biocompatibility holds great potential as an intelligent anti-microbial modality, particularly for chronic wound healing.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial infections are a severe threat to human population, global economy, and mental state of an individual [1]. For instance, outbreak of COVID-19 has frightened medical professionals all over the world, and clinical reports have indicated more fatality among COVID-infected patients from secondary infections or anti-microbial resistance (AMR) than the virus itself [2,3]. As bacteria contact the exposed area of skin surface, they inhabit in the exudative fluid and necrotic tissues and induce biofilm formation at an alarming rate, which is usually accelerated in diabetic patients [4]. Wound healing consists of complex physiological processes involving hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling [5]. Clinically, antibiotics, hydrogels, or skin disinfectants are administered for infectious wound healing [6–8], however, they do not contribute to improving wound healing [9]. Skin disinfectants at higher dosage cause contact dermatitis and disrupt thyroid metabolism or endocrine system due to lower immunocompatibility [10]. On the other hand, hydrogels are limited by rapid dehydration rate and poor mechanical stability [11]. To promote wound healing process along with bacterial eradication, it is encouraged to exhibit anti-inflammatory effects. Production of oxidative stress and toxic enzymes causes severe tissue damage is often associated with up/down-regulation of cytokines. Thus, a combination of anti-microbial and anti-inflammatory behavior is vital for developing novel therapeutics for wound healing purposes.

Till date, various metal-based nanomaterials have been reported to kill bacteria primarily by inducing oxidative stress and membrane disruption [12–15]; however, their aqueous stability and released metal ions induced toxicity are still a major pursuit [16]. Alternatively, carbon-based nanomaterials especially carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as metal-free catalysts by virtue of their selective electron transfer and light-harvesting capabilities [17–23]. For instance, Shi et al. demonstrated that CDs synthesized from candle soot exhibit peroxidase-mimetic activity [24]. In addition, doping of heteroatoms (nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, etc.) further boost their intrinsic enzyme-mimicking performance to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) [25–27]. On the other hand, CDs with an ability to absorb broad-spectrum incident light hold great potential in the photocatalytic generation of ROS [28–31]. For example, the plant extract-derived magnesium/nitrogen-doped CDs degraded pollutant, as sunlight facilitated in situ generation of electron-hole pairs for catalyzing ROS formation [32]. Another report showed near-infrared light-induced hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) mediated oxidation of benzyl alcohol by CDs [33]. It is of great importance to achieve efficient and tunable optical properties of CDs to enable multi-step catalytic reactions in a cascade manner for the controlled production of ROS.

In our previous report, we showed the preparation of amphipathic CDs through simple pyrolysis of triolein [34]. Owing to dual hydrophobic and hydrophilic components, these CDs self-assembled themselves to form unilamellar liposome-like structures (CDsomes) in aqueous solution, which exhibit photoswitchable properties upon sequential irradiation with ultraviolet (UV) and green light. We explored the possible underlying mechanisms for the CDs' light-driven “on” and “off” states and interdependent fluorescence states for cascade-type catalytic reactions. At first, “on” state of CDsomes can be achieved upon UV irradiation, which can be switched to “off” state with green light irradiation. Based on the energy bandgap (
⁓
3.04 eV), it is thermodynamically favorable for CDsomes to catalyze the two-electron reduction of oxygen molecules into H2O2 under UV irradiation (“on” state). Then, green light irradiation brings the CDsomes in their “off” state, in which CDsomes can imitate peroxidase-like behavior to convert H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH). We proposed that CDsomes generate highly reactive electron-hole pairs to boost the H2O2 formation under UV irradiation and generate a metastable radical state (⋅CDsomes – or ⋅CDsomes +) upon subsequent green light irradiation for peroxidase-like activity. Light-driven cascade reactions for the generation of cytotoxic ROS were further employed for anti-microbial therapy. CDsomes showed excellent cell viability and insignificant hemolytic activities against mice skin cells and erythrocytes, respectively. As developed CDsomes inhibited broad-spectrum of bacteria including multi-drug resistant (MDR) bacteria under sequential light activation. We have further applied CDsomes loaded dressings to disinfect methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) induced infection on the wounds of diabetic mice. Microbial pathogens were efficiently eradicated in vivo with CDsomes coupled with sequential light treatment, mainly due to efficient internalization facilitated elevation in localized oxidative stress. Owing to the unique structure-function relationship of oleic acid in CDsomes, a remarkable anti-inflammatory response was observed. In addition, CDsomes possess angiogenic properties, which stimulate epithelial reconstruction and collagen formation to accelerate wound healing. The light-guided programmable CDsomes possess great potential for the treatment of infected wounds.

In a typical process, triolein (30 g) in a beaker was heated at 220 °C for 3 consecutive days in a Muffle furnace [34]. After heating, the residue was resuspended in sodium hydroxide (NaOH; 0.2 M) and sonicated for 3 h. The CDsomes were collected after dialysis (MWCO = 15 kD) against phosphate-buffered saline (pH 7.4; PBS; containing 2.0 mM KH2PO4, 10 mM Na2HPO4, 137 mM NaCl, and 5.0 mM KCl) solution for 10 h, replaced with PBS every 2 h. Dialyzed CDsomes were stored at 4 °C when not in use. The yield of CDsomes was determined to be
⁓
30%, with respect to the mass of precursor.

Portable UV (385 nm) and green (532 nm) LEDs purchased from (SMALL SUN, China) were employed to assess the production of ROS through CDsomes. To measure H2O2, the CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) dispersed in 5.0 mM Tris–borate buffer (pH 7.0) were irradiated with UV light (385 nm, 2 mW cm−2) for 0–30 min. To which, a mixture of horseradish peroxidase (HRP; 0.1 U mL−1) and Amplex red (AR, 20.0 μM) was added prior to incubation for 2 h under dark at room temperature. Then, 200 μL from each sample was transferred into 96-well bottom plate and their fluorescence intensities were measured using a Synergy 4 monochromatic microplate spectrometer (Biotek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA) at excitation and emission wavelengths of 540 and 580 nm, respectively.

The capacity of CDsomes to generate hydroxyl radical (⋅OH) was investigated using terephthalic acid (TA) as a probe. The aliquots containing pre-irradiated (385 nm, 2 mW cm−2, 10 min) CDsomes in 5 mM Tris-borate buffer (pH 7.0) were further irradiated with green light (532 nm, 150 mW cm−2) for 0–5 min and incubated with TA (2 mM) under dark at room temperature for 1 h. The fluorescence intensity of each solution was measured at excitation and emission wavelength of 312 and 425 nm, respectively, using the aforementioned instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound healing of diabetic mice and histology
ce:section.ce:para: The wound healing studies were conducted using diabetic mouse model. All the animal experiment protocols were in accordance and approved by Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the National Laboratory Animal Center (NLAC; Permit No. IACUC2012–037). A gas mixture, containing Xylazine (117 mg kg−1) and Zoletil (5 mg kg−1), was used to anesthetize the BKS.Cg–7m+/+ Leprdb/J (db/db; Type 2 diabetes mellitus) male mice weighing (ca. 30–35 g). The dorsal skin of the mouse was trimmed and then wiped with 70% ethanol for aseptic surgery. The circular-shaped wounds (1 cm2) were made by incision on the back of the mice using sterile stainless steel scissors. 10 μL of MRSA [1 × 107 CFU] solution was poured onto the wound site and covered with a sterilized cotton gauze presoaked in PBS for cross contaminant-free bacterial growth. After 48 h, the wound site was swabbed and cultured on LB agar plates for 8 h at 37 °C to conﬁrm the MRSA infection. To assess the in vivo anti-microbial activity of CDsomes under different light responses, the CDsomes solution (100 μg mL−1) was irradiated with UV light (385 nm, 2 mW cm−2, 10 min) which activates CDsomes via the production of H2O2. Subsequently, the activated solution (20 μL) was applied on the wound site, and green laser light (532 nm, 150 mW cm−2, 1 min) was then irradiated on CDsomes-treated wound sites. The CDsomes treatment was performed every 12 h daily for 12 consecutive days. The tissue fluids were collected on postoperative day 9 and grown on LB agar plates to observe bacteria count through colony formation assay. Infected wounds treated with commercial antibacterial bandage i.e., 3 M served as a control group. In addition, digital photographs of the wounds were captured to monitor the day-to-day progress in the healing process. The relative wound area was calculated using the following equation:
Relative wound area (%) = A t/A 0 × 100, where A 0 and A t are the wound area on day 0 and at a specific day, respectively.

Histological evaluations of the wound tissues were studied by Masson's trichrome and Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, respectively. Tissue samples of the wound site including dermis and subcutaneous tissue were excised and stored in 10% formaldehyde solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo anti-inflammatory properties of light-activated CDsomes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate in vitro anti-inflammatory response, we employed lipopolysaccharides (LPS) induced inflammation to evaluate RAW264.7 anti-inflammatory responses of CDsomes under different light irradiation. Firstly, RAW264.7 macrophages (1 × 105 cell per well) cultured in DMEM (10% FBS) were seeded in a 96-well plates in 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, cells were co-incubated with LPS (5.0 μg mL−1) and UV light (385 nm, 2 mW cm−2) activated CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) followed by green light (532 nm, 150 mW cm−2) for 1 min and then incubated for another 18 h at 37 °C. Afterwards, pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin (IL) 10, IL-1β, and transforming growth factor β1 (TGF-β1) from RAW264.7 cells were quantified using ELISA kits as per the standard manufacturer's protocols. All samples were assayed in triplicates.

To perform immunohistochemical analysis of the wounds, the tissue specimen was fixed, punched, and washed with 4% paraformaldehyde, 0.3% Triton X–100, and 0.6% H2O2 (in methanol), respectively. Subcutaneous resected tissues were blocked with 5% bovine serum albumin (BSA) dispersed in PBS solution, and then reacted with the intended primary antibodies at a certain ratio against the M1 marker CD86 (anti-mouse 1:100) and the M2 marker CD206 (anti-rat 1:200) for 12 h at 4 °C. Subsequently, HRP-conjugated immunoglobulins G (IgG) secondary antibodies (anti-rabbit 1:100) were incubated for 30 min at room temperature. Tissue sections were washed three times with PBS solution (pH 7.4) to remove unbound reagents. Carefully washed tissue sections were covered with diaminobenzidine tetrahydrochloride solution (DAB; 2.5 mL DI water, 1 drop of H2O2, 250 μL substrate buffer, and 2 drops liquid DAB chromogen) and incubated for 5 min at room temperature. After continuous rinsing with sterile DI water for 5 min, the tissue slides were dipped in Harris's modified Hematoxylin with acetic acid for approximately 90 s. Eventually, the slides were washed with DI water after counterstaining and observed by an inverted fluorescent microscope (Olympus X70, Olympus, Japan).

For quantifying the levels of cytokines on the wound site, animals were euthanized from the overdose of anesthesia, and the tissue slice containing wounded and healthy skin were frozen in liquid nitrogen. Upon thawing, specimens (100 mg tissue mL−1) were homogenized using a mixture of phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (PMSF; 0.1 mM), NaCl (400 mM), BSA (0.5%), Tween 20 (0.05%), EDTA (10 mM), benzethonium chloride (0.1 mM), and aprotinin (20 KI; ∼33 μg mL−1). Each of the suspensions was then centrifuged (RCF 10,000 g, 10 min, 4 °C), and supernatants were collected to quantify the concentration of IL-10, IL-1β, and TGF-β1 using ELISA kits as per the standard manufacturer's protocols. All samples were assayed in four replicates.

Statistics. The results were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation. Comparative studies of the means were performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Significance was accepted at P < 0.05.

See the Supplementary Information file for the details of the materials, characterization of CDsomes, bacterial staining, ROS assays, bacterium membrane potential assays, in vitro cytotoxicity assays, and hemolysis assays.

Controlled pyrolysis of the triolein, and subsequent hydrolysis resulted in the formation of amphipathic carbon dots (CDs), as reported in our previous report [34]. Post-treatment, lyophilized CDs were resuspended in PBS to yield self-assembled CD liposomes (CDsomes) (Fig. 1 A). Surface of the CDs are partially and asymmetrically decorated with hydrophobic chains of the precursors, which led to the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity-guided development of liposome-like nanostructures. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum revealed self-preserved functional moieties (C=O, C–H, and C-OH) within CDsomes after mild carbonization (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). The intense D and G band corresponding to sp [2]-graphitic carbon in the Raman spectra and X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (2θ = 22.2°) confirms the graphitic nature of the CDs (Figs. S2A and S2B). Furthermore, C1s peak showed the dominance of C=C/C–C (62.1%) in the X-ray photoelectron (XPS) measurement of CDsomes (Fig. S3, Supporting Information), which corroborates with Raman and XRD analysis. Zeta potential (ζ) of CDsomes (1.0 mg mL−1) dispersed in PBS (pH 7.4) solution was measured as −31.4 ± 3.2 mV (n = 3, data not shows). Further, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) indicated the presence of [OA−H]− (20% by mass; OA: oleic acid) in CDsomes (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). Hydrodynamic diameters of carbon dots and self-assembled CDsomes (1.0 mg mL−1) were recorded in 50% ethanol (9.6 ± 7.2 nm) and PBS (98.8 ± 55.2) through dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. S5, Supporting Information).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images show the presence of liposome-like structure of CDsomes within the size range of 80–100 nm in PBS solution (Fig. 1B). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDsomes exhibits a distinguishable absorbance peak at 240 nm attributed to the π→π* transitions of graphitic carbon and a shoulder at 290 nm ascribing to the aromatic n→π* transitions of C=O bonds (Fig. 1C) [35]. The CDsomes exhibit maximum fluorescence intensity at 482 nm when excited at 400 nm (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). Their fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDsomes calculated using quinine sulfate (QY; 54% in 0.5 M H2SO4) as reference is 4.12%. Similar to most reported CDs [36], CDsomes display an obvious excitation-dependent fluorescence, probably due to their existence of varying sizes of polycyclic aromatic clusters, edge defects, surface emissive traps, and wider particle size distribution (Figure S6 and Fig. 1D) [37–39]. Interestingly, the as-prepared CDsomes are highly photostable (Fig. S7A, Supporting Information ). The morphology, particle size, and distribution remained unchanged after light irradiation (Figs. S7B and S7C, Supporting Information), revealing the structure of CDsomes is stable even after sequential UV light (385 nm, 25 mW cm−2, 10 min) and green light (532 nm, 150 mW cm−2, 1 min) irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic and peroxidase-imitating activities of CDsomes
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence of CDsomes can be reversibly switched between a fluorescent on- and non-fluorescent off-state under photoirradiation at 360–390 nm and 525–545 nm, respectively (Fig. 1E). The CDsomes show blue emission under the UV light excitation at 360–390 nm (on-state). On the other hand, they show an instant red emission after 1 s of green light (525–545 nm) excitation, which gets switched off after 40 s of irradiation. Importantly, the fluorescence signal can be restored (switched on) upon UV light (360–390 nm) excitation for 10 s. CDs have been reported to possess a large number of emissive and non-emissive traps on their surfaces [40,41]. It has been suggested that the off-state CDs can be switched back from the dark radical state to a bright fluorescent emissive state after the electron transfer from the neighboring high energy traps, especially for the self-assembled CDs. The closed CD self-assemblies (i.e., CDsomes) further facilitate mutual electron transfer to take place [40,41]. Furthermore, fluorescence “on” and “off” state-dependent catalytic properties of CDsomes under photoirradiation [UV (385 nm) and green (532 nm)] were investigated (Fig. 2 ). Generation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) by CDsomes in aqueous solution at room temperature was investigated using Amplex red (AR)/horseradish peroxidase (HRP) assays [42]. H2O2 catalyzed by HRP further reacts with AR in 1:1 stoichiometry ratio to produce highly fluorescent AR oxidized products i.e., resorufin (7-hydroxy-3H-phenoxazin-3-one with a QY of 0.83; excitation/emission maxima = 570/585 nm) [42]. As shown in Fig. 2A, CDsomes induced the generation of a high concentration of H2O2 with UV light irradiation (385 nm, 2 mW cm−2, 10 min), while having no obvious H2O2 formation either under 532 nm light irradiation or dark conditions. As prepared CDsomes formed an equivalent amount of H2O2 (
⁓
10 μM) as that from the reaction of AR, H2O2, and HRP, which is 20-fold higher than those incubated in the dark. This finding depicted that CDsomes exhibited photocatalytic activity in the “on” state, triggered by UV light. In addition, a slightly lower amount of H2O2 (
⁓
7.6 μM) was generated when the solution was purged with N2 (Fig. S8, Supporting Information), implying that the H2O2 was generated mainly from the reduction reaction of dissolved O2.

Additionally, to evaluate the peroxidase-like activity we employed non-fluorescent disodium terephthalate (NaTA) that reacts with ·OH to yield a blue fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA; excitation/emission maxima = 312/426 nm). In the presence of H2O2, the generation of the fluorescent HTA product was detected by 530 nm light irradiation (Fig. 2B). A similar trend was displayed when CDsomes were sequentially irradiated with UV light (385 nm, 2 mW cm−2, 10 min) and then green light (532 nm, 150 mW cm−2, 1 min) in the absence of H2O2. In contrast, no significant HTA fluorescence was observed for CDsomes either in the dark or under UV irradiation. Our results suggested that, “on” state CDsomes photo-catalytically generate H2O2 upon UV irradiation, followed by converting them to peroxidase-mimicking CDsomes (i.e., “off” state) in response to green light irradiation. Subsequently, the off-state CDsomes reacted with the generated H2O2 to form ·OH. Therefore, the photo-controllable cascade reaction enables the generation of ROS. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy measurements were carried out using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap to confirm the photo-controllable catalytical cascade reactions (Fig. 2C). Compared to dark, CDsomes irradiated with UV (385 nm) light exhibited a typical quartet (1:2:2:1) signal attributed to DMPO-·OH adducts. The high-intensity signal disappeared in the presence of catalase, indicating H2O2 was produced. After sequential irradiation with UV (385 nm) and green (532 nm) light, signal ascribing to ·OH became more prominent, further supporting the arguments of multi-step cascade-type reactions.

The features of an efficient photocatalyst are the broad visible light-absorption profile, faster charge transfer, and large surface area for redox reactions [43]. UV light irradiation-generated electron-hole pairs could migrate to the catalyst surface to participate in the photoassisted redox reaction. The optical band gap (Eg
⁓
3.07) of CDsomes was determined using the conventional Tauc plot approach obtained from UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. S9, Supporting Information), revealing that 385-nm exciting light can provide sufficient energy to drive the formation of H2O2 [44]. Moreover, electrochemical measurements of CDsomes were conducted using cyclic voltammetry to obtain current-voltage curves in a wide potential window (Fig. 2D(a)). The onset potential of the peaks allowed us to determine the position of valence band (EVB; 1.38 eV) and conduction band (ECB; −1.61 eV) of the CDsomes. The energy difference (2.99 eV) fits with the optical band gap obtained from the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S9). Furthermore, we evaluated the photocurrent responses of CDsomes in the absence of light and after excitation at 385 nm (2 mW cm−2) and 532 nm (150 mW cm−2), respectively. As shown in Fig. S10 (Supporting Information), a significant photocurrent was generated by the CDsomes (
⁓
1.7 × 10−6 A) under 385 nm light irradiation, while no obvious signal was observed under dark or 532 nm light. The amplified photo-induced current under UV light can be attributed to the semiconductor-like behavior of CDsomes and optical energy overlap in the near UV region (385 nm, 3.22 eV). Usually, flat band potentials determine the capacity of an electron and hole to drive certain possible reduction/oxidation reactions [41]. As shown in Fig. 2D(b), it is thermodynamically favorable for CDsomes to promote the photocatalytic formation of H2O2 from dissolved oxygen (O2) upon UV light illumination. The absence of O2˙– sextet (via two-step; O2 + e– → O2˙–, O2˙– + e– + 2H+ → H2O2) in the ESR spectra suggested the formation of H2O2 via one-step reduction (i.e., O2 + 2e– + 2H+ → H2O2 ΔE = 0.68 V). We observed the endoperoxide structures in the CDsomes at 841/872 (νC–OO) and 1094 (νC–O) cm−1 in the Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum after being irradiated with UV (385 nm) light (Fig. S11, Supporting Information). The formation of endoperoxide suppresses one-electron reduction of O2 to form O2˙– [45,46], which is consistent with our observation. Thus, we believe graphene-like motif in CDsomes photocatalytically generates the H2O2 from O2 mainly through the formation of endoperoxide scaffolds across the aromatic ring domains and subsequent release of H2O2. The reported CDs consist of various unique surface moieties, such as keto, aldehyde, and hydroxyl functional group with enhanced redox or photo-redox capabilities mainly due to their heterogeneous active reaction centers formed during pyrolysis [47–50]. The “on” and “off” states of carbon dots are usually associated with the change in their redox status [51–56]. However, the ESR spectrum of our CDsomes shows a strong signal at g = 1.997 upon green (532 nm) light irradiation, indicating that long-lived intermediate radicals (⋅CDsomes – or ⋅CDsomes +) exist in the “off” state, which is a dark state (Fig. S12, Supporting Information) [51–55]. In contrast, no such peaks were detected in either dark or under UV and longer-wavelength light (585, 625 or 808 nm) irradiation. Our results indicated only 532-nm light irradiation led the CDsomes to form an intermediate radical state (⋅CDsomes – or ⋅CDsomes +). The proposed energy band diagram of the CDsomes and its “on” and “off” states that assisted the formation of ROS is shown in Fig. 2E. Generally, the conjugated double bonds, the abundance of functional groups (e.g., phenol, epoxy, and carbonyl), and/or surface defects of CDs are associated with the generation of radicals [51–55]. On the other hand, it is reported that H2O2 can be the fluorescence switch to modulate the “on” and “off” states of the CDs [56]. As shown in Fig. S13 (Supporting Information), fluorescence quenching of CDsomes under green (532 nm) light irradiation will be regained after the addition of H2O2. Therefore, we hypothesized that H2O2 could behave as an electron acceptor to rescue the CDsomes trapped in the “off” state. It is also assumed that H2O2 can be converted into ⋅OH and OH− until ⋅CDsomes – or ⋅CDsomes + (metastable state) reaches the ground state, and these processes can proceed until H2O2 is completely consumed (Fig. 2E). Alternatively, UV irradiation-induced transition into second-order excited singlet state could also rescue the “off” state CDsomes like photoswitchable organic dyes [57–59].

By taking the advantage of enhanced and programmable ROS generation (Fig. 2B and C) and stability in various biological media (Fig. S14; Supporting Information), the anti-microbial effect of the CDsomes on the pathogenic microbes was studied (Fig. 3 A). Before evaluating the anti-microbial effects of the CDsomes, we assessed the cytotoxicity of CDsomes against human skin cells (i.e., HaCaT cell lines) and erythrocytes in the dark and under photoirradiation (Fig. S15, Supporting Information). In general, 385 nm light is harmful to human cells [60]. However, exposing the CDsomes treated cells to UV light indicated sufficient cell viability because very low power (25 mW cm−2) was applied. CDsomes with lipophilic properties were instantly engulfed (
⁓
10 s) as indicated in the microscopic fluorescence images of bacteria. Bacterial colony formation assay was performed to evaluate in vitro bactericidal properties of CDsomes (Fig. 3B). E. coli suspension treated with CDsomes in either dark (308 ± 26 CFU, n = 3) or individually under UV light (198 ± 9 CFU, n = 3) and green light (243 ± 26 CFU, n = 3) showed obvious bacterial growth, while negligible colonies were observed under sequential treatment (UV light for 10 min and green light for 1 min). MIC90 values (minimal inhibitory concentration required to kill at least 90% of the bacterial population) of the CDsomes were determined against different bacterial strains using a standard broth dilution method (Fig. 3C). Without any photo-treatment MIC90 values of CDsomes against Gram-positive and Gram-negative type bacterial strains were determined to be 1.7–2.5 μg mL−1 and >1000 μg mL−1, respectively. UV light irradiation reduced the MIC90 values against Gram-negative bacteria, while green light irradiation indicated no obvious therapeutic response. Interestingly, sequential UV and green light irradiation exhibited the most effective bacteriostatic activity and decreased the MIC90 values to 104.1–112.4 μg mL−1 against Gram-negative type bacterial strains. In control experiment, CDsomes irradiated with green light (532 nm, 150 mW cm−2, 1 min) did not exhibit any antibacterial effect (data not shown). Compared to human cells, bacteria are more susceptible to ROS due to their intolerance to oxidative stress [61]. Superior anti-microbial effect of CDsomes towards Gram-positive bacteria with ptoto-treatment can be attributed to the higher degree of cellular internationalization (Fig. S16, Supporting Information). The teichoic acids with lipid tail and zwitterion features on Gram-positive bacteria's cell wall are mainly attributed to CDsomes prone to access. Enhanced antibacterial activities of CDsomes upon sequential treatment to Gram-negative bacteria is mainly due to in situ photocatalytic cascade reactions-induced generation of ROS, which can disrupt the cell membrane and lyse the bacteria by severely damaging DNA, proteins or enzymes.

The impact of intracellular ROS generation contributing to the light-activated anti-microbial response was elucidated using CellROXDeep Red reagent assay (Fig. S17, Supporting Information) [62]. A non-fluorescent cell-permeant dye underwent oxidization by ROS to form a bright red fluorescent product with excitation/emission maxima at ∼644/665 nm. The bacteria-treated CDsomes caused a significant oxidative stress even without phototreatment. In addition, CDsomes induced a more pronounced ROS level in S. aureus relative to E. coli due to their higher cellular internationalization to S. aureus (Fig. S17). Compared to the green light only, bacteria treated with CDsomes exhibited higher ROS levels under UV light or sequential UV/green light irradiation. These findings support that CDsomes can efficiently participate in a light-initiated cascade catalytic reaction even in a complex biological system. The integrity of bacterial membrane in the presence of CDsomes was further assessed using 3,3′-diethyloxacarbocyanine (DiOC2; excitation/emission maxima 482/497 nm) staining method (Fig. S18, Supporting Information) [62]. The red fluorescence indicates DiOC2 accumulation in healthy membranes is accompanied by a shift from green to red emission due to dye stacking, therefore ratiometric analysis of red/green fluorescence (R/G) could be used to investigate the disruption of bacterial membrane in the system. Fig. S18B shows a decrease in R/G ratio when irradiated with a green light after UV exposure. These findings are in good agreement with the previous results, suggesting a direct relationship between ROS and cell death caused by the designed enzyme mimetics (i.e., CDsomes) under photoirradiation. Such precise control over the bacterial behavior from the external light source could open up a new class of remote-controlled nanozymes, that can circumvent the limitation of the natural enzymes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Programable catalytic CDsomes improve chronic wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: We assessed the wound healing efficacy of CDsomes in diabetic (db/db; Type 2 diabetes mellitus) mice (BKS.Cg–Dock7m +/+ Leprdb/J) infected with methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) (Fig. 4 ). Healthy mice with a wound size of 1 cm2 heals completely between 1 and 2 weeks, however it takes several weeks in diabetic db/db mice. No obvious change in wound size was observed in either of the mice until 6 days (Fig. 4A). In contrast to untreated (control) groups and CDsomes groups individually irradiated with UV or green light, sequential photoirradiation (UV + green light) of CDsomes-treated groups showed insignificant signs of edema and ulcers at the infection site on day 6 (Fig. 4A(a)). Compared to untreated groups (>80%), sequential photoirradiation (UV + green light) significantly reduced the relative wound size to 18% on day 9, which is apparently 2-fold smaller than those treated without photoirradiation (Fig. 4A(b)). To further demonstrate the anti-microbial activity of CDsomes, cultures collected from CDsomes treated wounds on day 9 indicated 187 ± 36 CFUs (n = 3) without any photoirradiation, while only 32 ± 7 CFUs (n = 3) were observed in the LB agar plates after sequential light irradiation (Fig. 4B). Although wound size after sequential light irradiation was only slightly smaller than that in the dark on day 6 (Fig. 4A(a)), the former drastically reduced the bacterial counts (Fig. 4B(b)). Effectively eliminating the infected bacteria by CDsomes coupled with sequential light irradiation accelerates the wound closing in the following days.

Histological studies were conducted on tissue specimens collected from the wound site on day 9 (Fig. 4C). Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining indicated a large number of immune cells (purple) at the infected wound site in the untreated groups. Among others, sequentially irradiated CDsomes (UV + green) alleviated the MRSA-induced inflammation, resulting in significant reduction of immune cells in the wound. Our results suggested that the UV irradiation initiated H2O2 and subsequent transformation into ⋅OH radicals can successfully mitigate the bacterial infection and associated inflammatory immune responses and tissue necrosis. Along with inflammatory, the proliferative phase is also protracted in chronic wounds mainly because pliant red blood cells (RBCs) regulate the oxygen level to the wound for collagen synthesis and tissue metabolism [63]. The collagen fibers were stained by Masson's trichrome staining, and blue color intensity displays the relative collagen level in the tissue specimens. Along with the bactericidal response, dual light-triggered CDsomes also encouraged faster wound healing by enhancing the epithelial restitution (black arrow) and collagen deposition (green rectangle). In addition, CDsomes under sequential treatment also promoted the development of hair follicles and reconstruction of granulation tissue by building the blood vessel network due to a sufficient supply of nutrients and oxygen (Fig. 4C).

Anti-inflammatory effects of photoirradiation of CDsomes was assessed qualitatively through immunostaining of CD86 and CD206 proteins (Fig. S19, Supporting Information). CD86 and CD206 protein is cytokine for M1 (pro-inflammatory) and M2 (anti-inflammatory) polarization of macrophage, respectively. Immunohistochemistry (IHC) analysis showed relatively insignificant M1 macrophages in the wounds treated with CDsomes under UV irradiation followed by green light, indicating that it is effective against bacteria-induced inflammation (Fig. S19A). A large number of M2 macrophages in the same group due to reduced inflammation and revascularisation contributed in the accelerated healing process. The CDsomes upon photo-activation not only eradicate microbial pathogenesis but also possess proangiogenic properties, which effectively promote tissue repair. Although inflammation is a part of the natural wound healing process, abnormal protein glycosylation and abnormal utilization of complement proteins owing to pathogenesis protracts the inflammatory phase, which leads to restrained chemotaxis [64]. Thus, regulation of pro- and/or anti-inflammatory cytokines levels is extremely vital during wound healing process [65]. ELISA analysis revealed that the level of pro-inflammatory Interleukin 1 Beta (IL-1β) was reduced, while the expressions of anti-inflammatory IL-10 and a housekeeping transforming growth factor-beta 1 (TGF-β1) were dramatically boosted in the sequentially irradiated CDsomes (UV + green) group (Fig. S19B). In addition to the suppressed bacterial colonization on the wound, the sequential light irradiation induced CDsomes release oleic acid to reduce the inflammation of the infected wound, because monounsaturated olive oil rich with oleic acid can reduce insulin resistance and inflammation by inducing M2 polarization of macrophages [66]. Moreover, oleic acid also inhibits NF-κB (essential protein complex which regulates transcription of DNA, and cytokine level) performance by activating adenosine monophosphate-activated protein kinase (AMPK) [67]. These findings are consistent with the in vitro LPS induced inflammation assays (Fig. S20, Supporting Information), further supporting the anti-inflammatory role of CDsomes.

Herein, we have demonstrated the one-pot pyrolytic synthesis of amphipathic CDs, which self-assembled into micellar-like structures (i.e., CDsomes) in an aqueous solution. As-prepared CDsomes were activated under light irradiation, and displayed a precise control over “on” and “off” states upon UV (385 nm) and green (532 nm) light exposure, respectively. The “on” state CDsomes effectively catalyzed H2O2 production due to UV light generated electron-hole pairs, which were further decomposed into ⋅OH by the “off” state peroxidase-mimic metastable states of CDsomes (⋅CDsomes – or ⋅CDsomes +) followed by green light irradiation. These programmable states of the CDsomes participated in a cascade reaction under sequential irradiation with UV and green light to eradicate broad-spectrum of bacteria, including MRSA. The MIC90 values of Gram-negative bacteria were reduced by
⁓
10-fold after sequential light treatment. CDsomes employed to treat the wounds of diabetic mice infected with MRSA coupled with sequential light irradiation effectively eradicated microbial pathogenesis from the infectious site and promoted angiogenesis, epithelization, and collagen deposition. In addition to the biocidal response, CDsomes encouraged rapid healing through regulation of the inflammatory immune responses by down/up mediating the pro/anti-inflammatory cytokines, attributed to the intrinsic properties of self-preserved oleic acid within CDsomes. Light triggered programmable and biocompatible CDsomes hold great potential as a smart anti-microbial modality for wound healing applications.

All data associated with this study are present in the paper or the Supplementary Materials. All raw data are available upon request by E-mail to: huanging@mail.ntou.edu.tw (C.-C. Huang), changht@ntu.edu.tw (H.-T. Chang).
